name: Area

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - TNPSC Aptitude Area Questions & Answers (real past-paper set)
2024_q page 31 Q44 (cylinder curved surface area, with answer key — found while researching an earlier topic, stashed and used here)

researchNotes: OCR search for 'area' combined with shape-keywords mostly returned noise (the word 'area' is too generic, appears constantly in GS/economy questions). The web-sourced set is itself drawn from real past papers across multiple years and covers a wide range of shapes (triangle-by-coordinates, rectangle-with-crossroads, sector-folded-into-cone, semicircle perimeter, grazing-horse/sector-of-circle, rhombus, circular-fencing-cost, carpet-cost) — picked the most distinct shapes to avoid redundant coverage. Expanded 2026-07 pass (append-only — also used Day 8, already completed): added trapezium, equilateral triangle, sector area (distinct from the existing sector-perimeter/grazing-horse questions), square-from-diagonal, circular ring/annulus, and circle-from-circumference shapes to round out coverage of the standard area-formula toolkit. Every question, new and pre-existing, now carries the mandatory Part-B `trick`. All arithmetic re-verified by hand. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): added parallelogram area, isosceles-triangle-via-Pythagoras, and running-track (rectangle+two semicircles=one circle) shapes — the last of the standard area-formula toolkit not yet covered. Caught and fixed a leftover placeholder token in an early draft of m18 before finalizing.

reading.intro: Area is 1 of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills. Real exam evidence shows a strong preference for applied/real-world framing (fencing cost, carpet cost, crossroads, a grazing horse) over bare formula plug-ins — and frequently combines area with a real-world cost-per-unit calculation.

qa.question: Method — area of a triangle from coordinates: Find the area of the triangle with vertices (0,0), (2,0), (0,2).
qa.answer: When two sides lie along the axes (a right triangle at the origin), area = ½ × base × height = ½ × 2 × 2 = 2 sq. units. (The general coordinate formula ½|x₁(y₂−y₃)+x₂(y₃−y₁)+x₃(y₁−y₂)| also works, but recognizing the right-angle-at-origin shortcut is faster.)

qa.question: Real exam: A rectangular ground 80m × 60m has two equal-width cross roads (one parallel to each side). Their combined area is 675 sq.m. Find the road width.
qa.answer: If width=w, the two roads' combined area = 80w + 60w − w² (subtracting the overlapping square where they cross, counted twice otherwise) = 140w − w². Set equal to 675: w² −140w+675=0. Solving gives w=5 (the other root, 135, is too large to be physically valid for an 80×60 ground).

qa.question: Real exam: Find the perimeter of a semicircle of radius 28cm.
qa.answer: Perimeter of a semicircle = πr + 2r (the curved half plus the straight diameter edge). Using π≈22/7: (22/7×28) + (2×28) = 88 + 56 = 144cm. (Common mistake: forgetting to add the straight diameter edge, which would wrongly give just 88cm.)

qa.question: Real exam: A horse is tethered by a 14m rope to one corner of a 60m×42m rectangular field. How much area is left UNGRAZED?
qa.answer: The horse can graze a quarter-circle of radius 14m (since it's tied at a corner, with two field-edges as boundaries): grazed area = ¼×π×14² = ¼×(22/7)×196 = 154 sq.m. Total field area = 60×42 = 2520 sq.m. Ungrazed = 2520−154 = 2366 sq.m.

qa.question: Real exam: Area of a rhombus with diagonals 6cm and 8cm?
qa.answer: Area of rhombus = ½ × d₁ × d₂ = ½×6×8 = 24 cm².

qa.question: Real exam: If fencing a circular park at ₹5/metre costs ₹1,100 total, find the park's radius.
qa.answer: Total fencing length (= circumference) = ₹1,100 ÷ ₹5/m = 220m. Circumference = 2πr → 220 = 2×(22/7)×r → r = 220×7/44 = 35m.

qa.question: Real exam: Find the cost of carpeting a room 13m long, 9m wide, using a carpet 75cm wide, at Rs.8 per metre (of carpet length).
qa.answer: Room area = 13×9 = 117 m². Carpet width = 0.75m, so length of carpet needed = Room area ÷ carpet width = 117/0.75 = 156m. Cost = 156 × Rs.8 = Rs.1,248.

qa.question: Real exam (related, surface area of a 3D shape): The curved surface area of a right circular cylinder of height 7cm is 66 cm². Find its diameter.
What is the formula for the area of a trapezium, and how do you apply it?
qa.answer: Area = ½ × (sum of the two parallel sides) × height. E.g., parallel sides 10cm and 14cm with height 6cm: ½×(10+14)×6 = ½×24×6 = 72 cm².
qa.tip: Trapezium area is a direct formula — no need to split it into a rectangle plus triangles unless you're deriving the formula itself.

qa.question: What is the formula for the area of an equilateral triangle, and what is it for a side of 8cm?
Method — area of a uniform-width path around a rectangular field: How do you find the area of a path built around (outside) a rectangular field?
qa.answer: Add twice the path's width to BOTH the length and breadth to get the outer rectangle's dimensions, find its area, then subtract the original (inner) field's area. Path area = Outer area − Inner area.

qa.question: Find the area of a 2m-wide path built around (outside) a 20m×15m rectangular field.
qa.answer: Outer dimensions = (20+2×2)×(15+2×2) = 24×19 = 456 m². Inner field area = 20×15 = 300 m². Path area = 456−300 = 156 m².

qa.question: What is the formula for the area of a sector of a circle, and how is it applied?
qa.answer: Sector area = (angle/360°) × π × r² — the fraction of the full circle's area corresponding to the sector's central angle. E.g., a 60° sector of a circle with radius 21cm: (60/360)×(22/7)×441 = (1/6)×1386 = 231 cm².

qa.question: Method — finding a circle's area when only its circumference is given: If a circle's circumference is 44cm, find its area.
qa.answer: First recover the radius: circumference=2πr → 44=2×(22/7)×r → r=44×7/44=7cm. Then Area=πr²=(22/7)×49=154 cm². Always find the radius first — never try to jump straight from circumference to area.

qa.question: Round-2 pass — What is the formula for the area of a parallelogram, and how does it apply to a parallelogram with base 12cm and height 7cm?
qa.answer: Area = base × height (using the PERPENDICULAR height, not a slanted side length). For base=12cm, height=7cm: Area = 12×7 = 84 cm².
qa.tip: A parallelogram's 'height' must be the perpendicular distance between the two parallel sides — never multiply base by the slanted side length directly.

qa.question: Method — area of an isosceles triangle from its equal sides and base (via the height, using Pythagoras): Find the area of an isosceles triangle with equal sides 13cm and base 10cm.
qa.answer: The height splits the base into two equal halves of 5cm. Height = √(13²−5²) = √(169−25) = √144 = 12cm. Area = ½×base×height = ½×10×12 = 60 cm².
qa.tip: For an isosceles triangle, drop the height from the apex to the midpoint of the base, forming a right triangle with the equal side as hypotenuse and half the base as one leg — solve for height via Pythagoras first.

qa.question: Method — area of a running track (a rectangle with a semicircle attached at each end): A running track consists of a rectangular middle section 80m long and 14m wide, capped by a semicircle of radius 7m at each end. Find the track's total area.
qa.answer: Rectangle area = 80×14 = 1,120 m². The two semicircles together make one full circle of radius 7m: Area = πr² = (22/7)×49 = 154 m². Total track area = 1,120+154 = 1,274 m².
qa.tip: Two semicircles of the same radius, one at each end of a rectangle, always combine into exactly one full circle — compute that circle's area once rather than each semicircle separately.

questions.prompt: Find the area of the triangle with vertices (0,0), (5,0), (0,4).
questions.explanation: Right triangle at origin: area = ½×5×4 = 10 sq. units.
questions.trick: When two vertices lie on the axes with the third at the origin, area = ½×base×height directly — skip the general coordinate formula.

questions.prompt: Find the perimeter of a semicircle of radius 21cm (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: Semicircle perimeter = πr + 2r — the '+2r' straight diameter edge is the most commonly forgotten term.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a rhombus with diagonals 10cm and 12cm.
questions.trick: Area of rhombus = ½×d1×d2 — a direct formula, no need to split into triangles.

questions.prompt: Fencing a circular garden at ₹4 per metre costs ₹880 in total. Find the garden's radius (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: Circumference = total cost ÷ cost-per-metre; then r = circumference/(2π).

questions.prompt: Find the cost of carpeting a room 10m long and 8m wide with a carpet 80cm wide, at Rs.10 per metre.
questions.explanation: Room area = 80 m². Carpet length needed = 80/0.8 = 100m. Cost = 100×Rs.10 = Rs.1,000.
questions.trick: Carpet length = Room area ÷ carpet width — convert the carpet's width to metres first if given in cm.

questions.prompt: A horse is tethered by a 7m rope to a corner of a 20m×15m rectangular field. Find the grazed area (use π≈22/7).
questions.explanation: Quarter circle of radius 7m: ¼×(22/7)×49 = 38.5 m².
questions.trick: A rope tied at a rectangle's corner sweeps a quarter-circle (90°) — use ¼×π×r², independent of the field's own dimensions as long as the rope is shorter than both sides.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a trapezium with parallel sides 10cm and 14cm, and height 6cm.
questions.trick: Area of trapezium = ½×(sum of parallel sides)×height — a direct formula.

questions.prompt: Find the area of an equilateral triangle with side 8cm.
questions.trick: Area of equilateral triangle = (√3/4)×side² — memorize directly rather than using Heron's formula.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a 2m-wide path built around (outside) a 20m×15m rectangular field.
questions.trick: Path area = (outer rectangle, with 2×width added to both dimensions) − (inner rectangle) — always add the width to BOTH length and breadth.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a sector with radius 21cm and central angle 60° (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: Sector area = (angle/360)×πr² — the angle's fraction of the full circle's area.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a square whose diagonal is 14cm.
questions.trick: Area of a square from its diagonal d = d²/2 — faster than finding the side length first.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a circular ring (annulus) with outer radius 14cm and inner radius 7cm (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: Area of a ring = π×(R²−r²) — subtract the radii-SQUARED, not the radii first.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a trapezium with parallel sides 12cm and 18cm, and height 10cm.
questions.trick: Same direct trapezium formula: ½×(sum of parallel sides)×height.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a triangle with base 18cm and height 10cm.
questions.trick: Area = ½×base×height — always try this most basic formula first.

questions.prompt: A rectangular field 100m×80m has a 5m-wide path built around it (outside). Find the path's area.
questions.trick: Same outer-minus-inner rectangle method as any path-around-a-field problem.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a circle whose circumference is 44cm (use π≈22/7).
questions.trick: Recover the radius from the circumference first (r=C/2π), then apply Area=πr² — never skip straight to area.

questions.prompt: Find the area of a parallelogram with base 15cm and height 8cm.
questions.trick: Area of a parallelogram = base × PERPENDICULAR height, never base × slanted side.

questions.prompt: Find the area of an isosceles triangle with equal sides 17cm and base 16cm.
questions.trick: Drop the height to the base's midpoint, then use Pythagoras (equal side as hypotenuse, half-base as one leg) to find the height before applying ½×base×height.

questions.prompt: A running track has a rectangular middle section 100m long and 20m wide, capped by a semicircle of radius 10m at each end. Find the track's total area (use π≈22/7).
questions.explanation: Rectangle = 100×20 = 2,000 m². Two semicircles of radius 10m = one full circle: (22/7)×100 = 314.3 m² (approx). Total ≈ 2,000+314.3 = 2,314.3 m².
questions.trick: Two equal semicircles at opposite ends of a rectangle = one full circle — compute once, not twice.

questions.prompt: Find the area of an isosceles triangle with equal sides 10cm and base 12cm.
questions.trick: Same Pythagoras-then-½×base×height approach as any isosceles-triangle area problem.
